summary: Julia Evans explains her unconventional approach to teaching programming concepts through self-published zines—short, illustrated booklets traditionally associated with punk and art movements. She argues that zines are an efficient teaching tool because they make intimidating technical content accessible and fun, allowing busy professionals to learn on their commute. The format's low barrier to entry—both for readers who find illustrated content less intimidating and for creators who don't need drawing skills—makes it an effective way to spread programming knowledge.
key_points: Zines transform intimidating technical material into approachable content that people actually read and learn from
The format is highly efficient: complex tools like tcpdump, strace, and eBPF can be meaningfully explained in a 24-page illustrated booklet
Even readers new to programming engage with advanced content because the format feels accessible rather than overwhelming
Zines are cheap to produce at scale ($300 for 500 copies), making them practical conference giveaways
Drawing skill is not a prerequisite—information organization matters more than artistic ability
money_quotes: So suddenly comics aren't just for fun– they're a tool that you can use to teach new ideas!
It turns out that most people don't have time to read long programming books. And a lot of useful ideas can be explained really quickly!
Fun, accessible content *works*. People understand it.
Because it's presented in an adorable illustrated 24-page zine people are like 'oh how interesting and cute!' and don't hesitate to pick it up, read it on their commute to work, and learn something.
I didn't understand 100% of it but a lot of it made sense!
When trying to teach, information content and organization is a lot more important than drawing skills.
key_insight: Making technical content visually fun and compact removes the intimidation barrier that keeps people from learning, proving that accessibility and depth aren't mutually exclusive.
